- Test fails then you fix it

- Novel (not already stored)
- Specific (clear guidance)
- Actionable (preferred, not mandatory)

**Workflow**: Search BEFORE deciding, capture AFTER learning.

# Beads Workflow Context

> **Context Recovery**: Run `bd prime` after compaction, clear, or new session
> Hooks auto-call this in Claude Code when .beads/ detected

# 🚨 SESSION CLOSE PROTOCOL 🚨

**CRITICAL**: Before saying "done" or "complete", you MUST run this checklist:

```
[ ] 1. git status              (check what changed)
[ ] 2. git add <files>         (stage code changes)
[ ] 3. git commit -m "..."     (commit code)
[ ] 4. git push                (push to remote)
```

**NEVER skip this.** Work is not done until pushed.

## Core Rules
- **Default**: Use beads for ALL task tracking (`bd create`, `bd ready`, `bd close`)
- **Prohibited**: Do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown files for task tracking
- **Workflow**: Create beads issue BEFORE writing code, mark in_progress when starting
- **Memory**: Use `bd remember "insight"` for persistent knowledge across sessions. Do NOT use MEMORY.md files — they fragment across accounts. Search with `bd memories <keyword>`.
- Persistence you don't need beats lost context
- Git workflow: beads auto-commit to Dolt, run `git push` at session end
- Session management: check `bd ready` for available work

## Essential Commands

### Finding Work
- `bd ready` - Show issues ready to work (no blockers)
- `bd list --status=open` - All open issues
- `bd list --status=in_progress` - Your active work
- `bd show <id>` - Detailed issue view with dependencies

### Creating & Updating
- `bd create --title="Summary of this issue" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task|bug|feature --priority=2` - New issue
  - Priority: 0-4 or P0-P4 (0=critical, 2=medium, 4=backlog). NOT "high"/"medium"/"low"
- `bd update <id> --claim` - Claim work
- `bd update <id> --assignee=username` - Assign to someone
- `bd update <id> --title/--description/--notes/--design` - Update fields inline
- `bd close <id>` - Mark complete
- `bd close <id1> <id2> ...` - Close multiple issues at once (more efficient)
- `bd close <id> --reason="explanation"` - Close with reason
- **Tip**: When creating multiple issues/tasks/epics, use parallel subagents for efficiency
- **WARNING**: Do NOT use `bd edit` - it opens $EDITOR (vim/nano) which blocks agents

### Dependencies & Blocking
- `bd dep add <issue> <depends-on>` - Add dependency (issue depends on depends-on)
- `bd blocked` - Show all blocked issues
- `bd show <id>` - See what's blocking/blocked by this issue

### Sync & Collaboration
- `bd dolt push` - Push beads to Dolt remote
- `bd dolt pull` - Pull beads from Dolt remote
- `bd search <query>` - Search issues by keyword

### Project Health
- `bd stats` - Project statistics (open/closed/blocked counts)
- `bd doctor` - Check for issues (sync problems, missing hooks)

## Common Workflows

**Starting work:**
```bash
bd ready           # Find available work
bd show <id>       # Review issue details
bd update <id> --claim  # Claim it
```

**Completing work:**
```bash
bd close <id1> <id2> ...    # Close all completed issues at once
git add . && git commit -m "..."  # Commit code changes
git push                    # Push to remote
```

**Creating dependent work:**
```bash
# Run bd create commands in parallel (use subagents for many items)
bd create --title="Implement feature X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=feature
bd create --title="Write tests for X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task
bd dep add beads-yyy beads-xxx  # Tests depend on Feature (Feature blocks tests)
```

○ long-running-harness-33z [EPIC] · E10: TR-808 Drum Machine   [● P1 · OPEN]
Owner: Nathan Delacrétaz · Type: epic
Created: 2026-03-28 · Updated: 2026-03-28

DESCRIPTION
Sample-based 808 (NOT analog synthesis, per advisory P0-5). 11 drum instruments with high-quality bundled one-shot samples: bass drum, snare, low/mid/high tom, rimshot, clap, cowbell, open hi-hat, closed hi-hat, cymbal. Per-instrument tweakable parameters: Tone (BiquadFilterNode cutoff), Decay (amplitude envelope release), Tune (AudioBufferSourceNode playback rate), Volume (GainNode gain). Sample playback via AudioBufferSourceNode + BiquadFilterNode + GainNode envelope. 16-step sequencer grid UI with per-step on/off toggle, accent toggle, flam timing control. Pattern A/B storage and switching. Step sequencer advances synchronized to transport clock via TransportClock subscription (R-STA-06). Audio output routed to dedicated drum track channel strip in mixer. 808 panel UI: pad grid (trigger on click), step sequencer row per instrument, per-instrument knob row (Tone/Decay/Tune/Volume). EARS: R-EVT-12, R-STA-06. Interface exports: DrumKit, DrumPattern, DrumTrigger. Research: docs/research/development/web-audio/synthesizer-dsp-fundamentals.md (Section 6: TR-808). QA: step timing accuracy (sample-accurate), accent/flam timing, parameter range.

DEPENDS ON
  → ✓ long-running-harness-5he: E2: Neo-Brutalist Design System ● P1
  → ✓ long-running-harness-8ew: E3: Transport & Scheduling Engine ● P0
  → ✓ long-running-harness-bqt: E7: Mixer Core ● P0
  → ✓ long-running-harness-dxr: E4: Media Pool & Audio Storage ● P1

BLOCKS
  ← ○ long-running-harness-aow: (EPIC) E17: Bounce/Export ● P1
  ← ○ long-running-harness-tnf: (EPIC) E18: Integration Verification ● P1

1	# Agent Instructions
2	
3	This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.
4	
5	## Project Map
6	
7	```
8	README.md             # Project overview
9	AGENTS.md             # YOU ARE HERE -- agent entry point
10	index.html            # Vite entry point
11	package.json          # Dependencies and scripts
12	src/
13	  main.tsx            # React entry point
14	  App.tsx             # Root component
15	  audio/              # Web Audio API engine (path alias: @audio)
16	  ui/                 # UI components (path alias: @ui)
17	  state/              # State management (path alias: @state)
18	  test/               # Test setup and utilities
19	docs/
20	  INDEX.md            # Documentation hub
21	  adr/                # Architectural Decision Records
22	  research/           # Deep research papers
23	  compound/           # Compound-agent documentation
24	.claude/
25	  CLAUDE.md           # Project instructions + agentic principles
26	  skills/             # Skill definitions (compound workflows)
27	  agents/             # Subagent definitions
28	  commands/           # Slash command definitions
29	  lessons/            # Session memory (NEVER edit directly -- use `ca` CLI)
30	.beads/               # Issue tracking data (Dolt-powered)
31	```
32	
33	## Stack
34	
35	- **React 19 + TypeScript 5.8** (strict mode, `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`, `exactOptionalPropertyTypes`)
36	- **Vite 8** for dev server and build
37	- **Vitest 4** for testing (jsdom environment, @testing-library/react)
38	- **ESLint 10** with typescript-eslint strict type-checked rules
39	- **Prettier** for formatting
40	- **Husky** pre-commit hooks (lint + format check)
41	- **GitHub Actions CI** (lint, format, test, build)
42	
43	## Build Commands
44	
45	```bash
46	pnpm dev              # Start dev server
47	pnpm build            # Type-check + production build
48	pnpm test             # Run tests once
49	pnpm test:watch       # Run tests in watch mode
50	pnpm test:coverage    # Run tests with coverage
51	pnpm lint             # ESLint + TypeScript noEmit
52	pnpm lint:fix         # Auto-fix lint issues
53	pnpm format           # Format source files
54	pnpm format:check     # Check formatting (CI)
55	pnpm check            # Full quality gate: lint + format + test
56	```
57	
58	## Path Aliases
59	
60	Use `@/`, `@audio/`, `@ui/`, `@state/` instead of deep relative imports. ESLint enforces this (no `../../../` allowed).
61	
62	## Conventions
63	
64	- **Issue tracking**: `bd` only. No markdown TODOs.
65	- **Session memory**: `ca search` before decisions, `ca learn` after discoveries.
66	- **Decisions**: Record in `docs/adr/` using the template. See [ADR-0001](docs/adr/0001-initial-tooling-choices.md).
67	- **Research**: Extensive docs in `docs/research/`. Send subagents there for context (`ca knowledge "topic"`).
68	- **Tests**: Write tests first (TDD). Tests live next to source files as `*.test.tsx`/`*.test.ts`.
69	- **Types**: Strict TS everywhere. No `any`. Explicit return types on functions.
70	
71	## Quick Reference
72	
73	```bash
74	bd ready              # Find available work
75	bd show <id>          # View issue details
76	bd update <id> --claim  # Claim work atomically
77	bd close <id>         # Complete work
78	bd dolt push          # Push beads data to remote
79	```
80	
81	## Non-Interactive Shell Commands
82	
83	**ALWAYS use non-interactive flags** with file operations to avoid hanging on confirmation prompts.
84	
85	Shell commands like `cp`, `mv`, and `rm` may be aliased to include `-i` (interactive) mode on some systems, causing the agent to hang indefinitely waiting for y/n input.
86	
87	**Use these forms instead:**
88	```bash
89	# Force overwrite without prompting
90	cp -f source dest           # NOT: cp source dest
91	mv -f source dest           # NOT: mv source dest
92	rm -f file                  # NOT: rm file
93	
94	# For recursive operations
95	rm -rf directory            # NOT: rm -r directory
96	cp -rf source dest          # NOT: cp -r source dest
97	```
98	
99	**Other commands that may prompt:**
100	- `scp` - use `-o BatchMode=yes` for non-interactive
101	- `ssh` - use `-o BatchMode=yes` to fail instead of prompting
102	- `apt-get` - use `-y` flag
103	- `brew` - use `HOMEBREW_NO_AUTO_UPDATE=1` env var
104	
105	<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION profile:full hash:d4f96305 -->
106	## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)
107	
108	**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.
109	
110	### Why bd?
111	
112	- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
113	- Git-friendly: Dolt-powered version control with native sync
114	- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
115	- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion
116	
117	### Quick Start
118	
119	**Check for ready work:**
120	
121	```bash
122	bd ready --json
123	```
124	
125	**Create new issues:**
126	
127	```bash
128	bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t bug|feature|task -p 0-4 --json
129	bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
130	```
131	
132	**Claim and update:**
133

145	### Issue Types
146	
147	- `bug` - Something broken
148	- `feature` - New functionality
149	- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)

153	### Priorities
154	
155	- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)

161	### Workflow for AI Agents

163	1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues

165	3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document

1	---
2	title: "Synthesizer Architecture and DSP Fundamentals for Browser-Based Audio Applications"
3	date: 2026-03-28
4	summary: "Survey of synthesizer architecture, DSP algorithms, and sound design patterns for implementing professional-grade software synthesizers using the Web Audio API and AudioWorklet, including the iconic TR-808 drum machine."
5	keywords: [synthesizer, dsp, web-audio, oscillators, filters, tr-808]
6	---
7	
8	# Synthesizer Architecture and DSP Fundamentals for Browser-Based Audio Applications
9	
10	*2026-03-28*
11	
12	## Abstract
13	
14	The Web Audio API, extended through AudioWorklet, has matured into a viable platform for professional-grade software synthesis. This survey examines the theoretical and practical landscape of digital signal processing (DSP) as applied to browser-based synthesizers, covering every major architectural layer from oscillator design through effects processing. The survey bridges classical DSP literature -- Zolzer, Smith, Puckette, Roads -- with the specific constraints and affordances of the browser audio graph: a 128-sample render quantum, a dedicated audio thread, and a JavaScript/WebAssembly processing model.
15	
16	The paper addresses ten interconnected domains: oscillator design including band-limited algorithms and wavetable synthesis; filter design spanning biquad, state-variable, and analog-modeled topologies; envelope generators with exponential and multi-stage curves; low-frequency oscillators with sync and tempo modes; modulation matrix architecture; the Roland TR-808 drum machine as a worked case study in analog synthesis emulation; arpeggiator patterns; polyphony management and voice allocation; preset serialization and morphing; and effects processing from convolution reverb through parametric EQ. Each domain receives mathematical foundations, algorithmic analysis, trade-off discussion, and concrete TypeScript/Web Audio API code patterns suitable for production implementation.
17	
18	The central finding is that browser-based synthesis can achieve professional quality when built around AudioWorklet-resident DSP rather than the coarse-grained native nodes, when band-limiting and anti-aliasing are applied systematically, and when the modulation system follows a clean source-amount-destination matrix that decouples routing from computation. Open problems include AudioWorklet WASM sharing constraints, consistent AudioParam scheduling latency, and the absence of native oversampling support in the Web Audio API specification.
19	
20	---
21	
22	## 1. Introduction
23	
24	### 1.1 Problem Statement
25	
26	Software synthesis in the browser faces a dual constraint: it must execute within the memory and compute budget of a shared JavaScript runtime while achieving sample-accurate audio quality indistinguishable from native desktop synthesizers. The Web Audio API (W3C, 2021) was designed for web audio effects, not for full synthesizer architectures, which means foundational DSP components -- oscillators, filters, envelopes, modulation routing -- must be implemented by the application developer, typically in AudioWorklet processors.
27	
28	The consequence is that browser synthesizer authors must simultaneously master three bodies of knowledge: classical analog synthesis theory, digital DSP algorithms, and the specific scheduling and threading model of the Web Audio API. The literature treats these separately. This survey integrates them.
29	
30	### 1.2 Scope
31	
32	**Covered:**
33	- Oscillator waveform generation and anti-aliasing (PolyBLEP, wavetable, additive)
34	- IIR filter design: biquad coefficients, state-variable filters, analog-modeled topologies
35	- Envelope and LFO architectures with modulation matrix
36	- TR-808 drum machine synthesis recipes and their Web Audio API implementations
37	- Arpeggiator pattern algorithms
38	- Polyphony management and voice lifecycle
39	- Preset serialization, storage, and morphing
40	- Effects chain: reverb, delay, distortion, modulation effects, dynamics, EQ
41	
42	**Excluded:**
43	- FM synthesis (Yamaha DX7 architecture) -- merits a separate survey
44	- Physical modeling synthesis (waveguide, finite-difference)
45	- Machine learning audio synthesis (RAVE, DDSP)
46	- MIDI protocol handling and device enumeration
47	- Streaming audio and Web RTC integration
48	
49	### 1.3 Key Definitions
50	
51	- **Sample rate (Fs)**: Samples per second; browser standard is 48000 Hz or 44100 Hz.
52	- **Nyquist frequency**: Fs/2; maximum representable frequency without aliasing.
53	- **Render quantum**: The atomic audio processing block in Web Audio API -- 128 samples.
54	- **AudioWorklet**: The W3C API for running custom DSP code on the audio rendering thread.
55	- **Partial**: A sinusoidal component of a complex waveform, at a harmonic or inharmonic frequency.
56	- **Aliasing**: Spectral fold-over when a signal contains energy above the Nyquist frequency.
57	- **Band-limited oscillator (BLO)**: An oscillator whose spectrum is bounded to [0, Nyquist].
58	
59	---
60	
61	## 2. Foundations
62	
63	### 2.1 Discrete-Time Signal Processing
64	
65	Audio in a digital system is represented as a sequence of real-valued samples x[n], where n is the sample index and the continuous-time signal x(t) is recovered via a reconstruction filter. The sampling theorem (Nyquist, Shannon) states that faithful reconstruction requires Fs > 2 * f_max, where f_max is the highest frequency in the signal.
66	
67	The z-transform maps the sample-domain to a complex frequency domain:
68	
69	```
70	X(z) = sum_{n=-inf}^{inf} x[n] * z^{-n}
71	```
72	
73	A filter described by H(z) transforms an input signal X(z) into output Y(z) = H(z) * X(z). IIR (infinite impulse response) filters implement this as a rational transfer function:
74	
75	```
76	H(z) = (b0 + b1*z^{-1} + b2*z^{-2}) / (1 + a1*z^{-1} + a2*z^{-2})
77	```
78	
79	which in the time domain corresponds to the difference equation:
80	
81	```
82	y[n] = b0*x[n] + b1*x[n-1] + b2*x[n-2] - a1*y[n-1] - a2*y[n-2]
83	```
84	
85	This is the Direct Form I biquad, the workhorse of all filter implementations in this survey.
86	
87	### 2.2 Frequency, Phase, and the Phase Accumulator
88	
89	A digital oscillator maintains a phase variable phi in [0, 1), incremented each sample by:
90	
91	```
92	delta_phi = f / Fs
93	```
94	
95	where f is the desired frequency. The phase wraps modulo 1. All waveform shapes are functions of phi:
96	
97	```
98	sine:     sin(2*pi*phi)
99	sawtooth: 2*phi - 1
100	square:   phi < duty ? 1.0 : -1.0
101	triangle: 4*|phi - 0.5| - 1
102	```
103	
104	The phase accumulator is O(1) per sample and is the basis for all oscillator implementations. The aliasing problem arises because sawtooth, square, and triangle waveforms have infinite-bandwidth Fourier series, generating harmonics that fold back into the audible band when computed naively.
105	
106	### 2.3 Decibels and Gain Staging
107	
108	Audio levels are expressed in decibels: dB = 20*log10(|amplitude|). The human auditory system perceives loudness approximately logarithmically, making dB natural for parameter control. In a synthesizer, gain staging ensures no stage clips the signal before it reaches a final limiter. Typical convention: oscillators output at 0 dBFS peak (amplitude 1.0), VCA attenuates before mixing, final output bus targets -6 to -12 dBFS headroom.
109	
110	### 2.4 Web Audio API Execution Model
111	
112	The Web Audio API processes audio on a dedicated audio rendering thread separate from the main JavaScript thread. AudioWorklet processors run on this thread with a guaranteed 128-sample block period. Communication with the main thread occurs via MessagePort (asynchronous, variable latency) or AudioParam automation (sample-accurate, scheduled ahead of time).
113	
114	AudioParams support two scheduling primitives relevant to synthesis:
115	- `setValueAtTime(value, time)`: instantaneous parameter change
116	- `linearRampToValueAtTime(value, endTime)`: linear interpolation
117	- `exponentialRampToValueAtTime(value, endTime)`: exponential interpolation
118	- `setTargetAtTime(target, startTime, timeConstant)`: exponential approach
119	
120	For synthesis-grade envelope control, custom AudioWorklet processors implement their own envelope state machines rather than relying on these AudioParam methods, because the Web Audio API methods have quantization to render quantum boundaries in some implementations.
121	
122	---
123	
124	## 3. Taxonomy of Approaches
125	
126	### 3.1 Synthesis Architecture Layers
127	
128	A software synthesizer can be decomposed into orthogonal layers:
129	
130	```
131	+--------------------------------------------------+
132	|  PRESET SYSTEM (serialization, morphing)         |
133	+--------------------------------------------------+
134	|  MODULATION MATRIX (source -> amount -> dest)    |
135	+--------------------------------------------------+
136	|  VOICE ENGINE (polyphony, voice allocation)      |
137	+--------------------------------------------------+
138	|  SIGNAL CHAIN PER VOICE                          |
139	|  [Oscillator(s)] -> [Filter] -> [VCA]            |
140	+--------------------------------------------------+
141	|  ENVELOPE / LFO GENERATORS (per voice / global) |
142	+--------------------------------------------------+
143	|  EFFECTS BUS (reverb, delay, distortion, etc.)  |
144	+--------------------------------------------------+
145	|  WEB AUDIO API / AudioWorklet SUBSTRATE         |
146	+--------------------------------------------------+
147	```
148	
149	### 3.2 Oscillator Algorithm Taxonomy
150	
151	| Algorithm       | Anti-alias | Quality   | CPU Cost | Pitch-agile |
152	|----------------|-----------|-----------|----------|-------------|
153	| Naive          | None      | Poor      | Minimal  | Yes         |
154	| PolyBLEP       | BLEP correction | Good | Low    | Yes         |
155	| BLIT           | Sinc summation | Very good | Medium | Yes      |
156	| Additive       | Perfect   | Perfect   | High     | Yes         |
157	| Wavetable      | Mipmapped | Very good | Low      | Limited     |
158	| MinBLEP        | Minimum-phase BLEP | Excellent | Medium | Yes  |
159	
160	### 3.3 Filter Topology Taxonomy
161	
162	| Topology            | Poles | Self-oscillation | Analog model   | CPU Cost |
163	|--------------------|-------|-----------------|---------------|----------|
164	| Biquad (Direct I)  | 2     | No              | Generic IIR   | Minimal  |
165	| State-Variable     | 2     | Yes (careful)   | Oberheim/EMS  | Low      |
166	| Moog Ladder        | 4     | Yes             | Moog Model D  | Medium   |
167	| Korg MS-20         | 2+NL  | Yes             | Sallen-Key    | Medium   |
168	| Chamberlin SVF     | 2     | Yes             | CEM3350       | Low      |
169	| Zavalishin ZDF     | 2-4   | Yes             | VA universal  | Medium   |

173	## 4. Analysis
174	
175	## 4.1 Oscillator Design
176	
177	### 4.1.1 Standard Waveforms and the Aliasing Problem
178	
179	The ideal sawtooth wave has the Fourier series:
180	
181	```
182	saw(phi) = (2/pi) * sum_{k=1}^{inf} (-1)^{k+1} * sin(2*pi*k*phi) / k
183	```
184	
185	At a fundamental of 440 Hz with Fs = 48000 Hz, the Nyquist limit (24000 Hz) allows harmonics up to k = 54. The naive sawtooth generates all harmonics including k > 54, which alias back into the audible band, creating spectral "mush" that worsens at higher pitches.
186	
187	Naive implementation in an AudioWorklet:
188	
189	```typescript
190	// AudioWorkletProcessor: NaiveOscillator
191	// WARNING: aliasing above ~2kHz fundamental
192	class NaiveOscillatorProcessor extends AudioWorkletProcessor {
193	  private phase = 0;
194	
195	  process(inputs: Float32Array[][], outputs: Float32Array[][], params: Record<string, Float32Array>): boolean {
196	    const output = outputs[0][0];
197	    const freqParam = params['frequency'];
198	
199	    for (let i = 0; i < output.length; i++) {
200	      const freq = freqParam.length > 1 ? freqParam[i] : freqParam[0];
201	      const delta = freq / sampleRate;
202	      output[i] = 2 * this.phase - 1; // sawtooth
203	      this.phase = (this.phase + delta) % 1;
204	    }
205	    return true;
206	  }
207	}
208	```
209	
210	### 4.1.2 PolyBLEP Anti-Aliasing
211	
212	PolyBLEP (Polynomial Bandlimited Step) corrects the discontinuity in sawtooth and square waveforms by adding a polynomial correction term near each transition point. The correction is computed analytically from the fractional position of the discontinuity within the current sample interval.
213	
214	The PolyBLEP correction for a falling edge at phase t (normalized, where 0 means the discontinuity just passed and 1 means one full cycle has elapsed) with step size dt:
215	
216	```
217	polyBlep(t, dt):
218	  if t < dt:      // just after transition
219	    t /= dt
220	    return t + t - t*t - 1.0
221	  elif t > 1 - dt: // just before transition
222	    t = (t - 1) / dt
223	    return t*t + t + t + 1.0
224	  else:
225	    return 0.0
226	```
227	
228	Applied to a sawtooth, the correction is subtracted at the wrap-around discontinuity. Applied to a square, it is applied at both the rising and falling edges.
229	
230	```typescript
231	// PolyBLEP sawtooth -- production quality up to ~8kHz fundamental at 48kHz
232	function polyBlepSaw(phase: number, dt: number): number {
233	  let saw = 2.0 * phase - 1.0;
234	  saw -= polyBlep(phase, dt);
235	  return saw;
236	}
237	
238	function polyBlep(t: number, dt: number): number {
239	  if (t < dt) {
240	    const x = t / dt;
241	    return x + x - x * x - 1.0;
242	  } else if (t > 1.0 - dt) {
243	    const x = (t - 1.0) / dt;
244	    return x * x + x + x + 1.0;
245	  }
246	  return 0.0;
247	}
248	```
249	
250	**Strengths:** O(1) per sample, no tables, pitch-agile, minimal latency.
251	**Limitations:** 1st-order correction leaves residual aliasing above approximately Fs/6; 2nd-order PolyBLEP2 improves this significantly.
252	
253	### 4.1.3 MinBLEP
254	
255	MinBLEP (Minimum-phase Bandlimited step) convolves a windowed sinc (the "ideal" step correction) with a minimum-phase spectral envelope, producing a compact impulse with all its energy concentrated at the front. This allows it to be stored as a short lookup table and applied at each discontinuity.
256	
257	The MinBLEP table is precomputed offline:
258	1. Create an oversampled sinc of length N (e.g., N = 64 * oversampling_factor)
259	2. Window it with a Blackman-Harris window
260	3. Apply a minimum-phase reconstruction via the cepstrum method
261	4. Store as a floating-point array
262	
263	At each discontinuity, the table is mixed into an output buffer at the fractional position of the discontinuity. This requires a small "residual buffer" per oscillator voice.
264	
265	**Strengths:** Near-perfect anti-aliasing; audibly transparent at all pitches up to Nyquist/2.
266	**Limitations:** Implementation complexity; residual buffer management; small per-sample overhead proportional to MinBLEP table length / period length.
267	
268	### 4.1.4 Wavetable Synthesis
269	
270	Wavetable synthesis stores one or more complete waveform cycles as arrays of floating-point samples. Playback reads the table at a rate proportional to the desired pitch. To prevent aliasing, multiple mipmapped versions of each wavetable are precomputed at different bandwidths: the version whose highest harmonic is below Nyquist for the current pitch is selected.
271	
272	**Table construction:**
273	1. Define the waveform in the frequency domain (Fourier coefficients).
274	2. For each mipmap level m, zero all harmonics above Fs / (2 * pitch_range_bottom_of_level_m).
275	3. IFFT back to time domain.
276	4. Store tables contiguously in a flat `Float32Array`.
277	
278	```typescript
279	interface WavetableBank {
280	  tables: Float32Array[];    // one per mipmap level
281	  tableSizes: number[];      // each table's sample count
282	  minFreqs: number[];        // minimum pitch for each table
283	}
284	
285	function readWavetable(bank: WavetableBank, freq: number, phase: number, sampleRate: number): number {
286	  // Select mipmap level
287	  let level = 0;
288	  for (let i = 0; i < bank.minFreqs.length; i++) {
289	    if (freq >= bank.minFreqs[i]) level = i;
290	  }
291	  const table = bank.tables[level];
292	  const size = bank.tableSizes[level];
293	
294	  // Linear interpolation between adjacent samples
295	  const readPos = phase * size;
296	  const idx0 = Math.floor(readPos) % size;
297	  const idx1 = (idx0 + 1) % size;
298	  const frac = readPos - Math.floor(readPos);
299	  return table[idx0] + frac * (table[idx1] - table[idx0]);
300	}

303	**Wavetable morphing:** Crossfade between two wavetables by linear interpolation of their outputs:
304	
305	```typescript
306	const morphedSample = (1 - morphAmount) * readWavetable(bankA, ...) + morphAmount * readWavetable(bankB, ...);
307	```
308	
309	For smooth spectral morphing, morphing in the frequency domain (interpolate Fourier coefficients before IFFT) produces cleaner results than time-domain crossfading.
310	
311	**Strengths:** Low CPU per voice; supports arbitrary complex waveforms; morphing enables expressive timbre evolution.
312	**Limitations:** Wavetable precomputation cost; memory for mipmap banks; pitch resolution limited by table count.
313	
314	### 4.1.5 Additive Synthesis
315	
316	Additive synthesis sums sinusoidal partials, each with independent amplitude and frequency:
317	
318	```
319	y[n] = sum_{k=1}^{K} A_k * sin(2*pi*f_k*n/Fs + phi_k)
320	```
321	
322	Because each partial is a sine (band-limited by definition), additive synthesis is inherently alias-free. The Fourier series of a sawtooth at 440 Hz requires 54 partials at 48 kHz -- feasible but O(K) per sample.
323	
324	**Resynthesis workflow:** Analyze an audio recording with an STFT, track partial amplitudes and frequencies over time, store as a time-varying additive model, resynthesize at any pitch.
325	
326	**Strengths:** Perfect quality; amenable to spectral transformation.
327	**Limitations:** High CPU for complex timbres; analysis tools required for resynthesis.
328	
329	### 4.1.6 Noise Generators
330	
331	**White noise** is generated by a pseudo-random number generator (PRNG) with a flat spectral density. A linear congruential generator (LCG) or xoshiro256** are suitable for audio:
332	
333	```typescript
334	// Xoshiro128+ -- fast, high-quality white noise
335	class WhiteNoise {
336	  private state = new Uint32Array([1, 2, 3, 4]);
337	  next(): number {
338	    const s0 = this.state[0], s1 = this.state[1];
339	    const result = (s0 + this.state[3]) >>> 0;
340	    this.state[1] ^= s0;
341	    this.state[3] ^= this.state[2];
342	    this.state[2] ^= s1;
343	    this.state[0] ^= this.state[3];
344	    this.state[3] = ((this.state[3] << 11) | (this.state[3] >>> 21)) >>> 0;
345	    return (result >>> 8) * (1.0 / 16777216.0) * 2 - 1;
346	  }
347	}
348	```
349	
350	**Pink noise** (1/f spectral density, -3 dB/octave) is perceptually "neutral" colored noise. The Voss-McCartney algorithm uses a bank of white noise generators at octave-spaced update rates, summed together. Paul Kellet's 3-coefficient IIR approximation is computationally preferable:
351	
352	```typescript
353	// Kellet pink noise filter (3 coefficients)
354	let b0 = 0, b1 = 0, b2 = 0;
355	function pinkNoise(white: number): number {
356	  b0 = 0.99886 * b0 + white * 0.0555179;
357	  b1 = 0.99332 * b1 + white * 0.0750759;
358	  b2 = 0.96900 * b2 + white * 0.1538520;
359	  return (b0 + b1 + b2 + white * 0.0168980) * 0.11;
360	}
361	```
362	
363	**Brown noise** (1/f^2 spectral density, -6 dB/octave) is a simple one-pole IIR on white noise:
364	
365	```typescript
366	let brownState = 0;
367	function brownNoise(white: number): number {
368	  brownState = (brownState + 0.02 * white) / 1.02;
369	  return brownState * 3.5; // normalize approximate peak
370	}
371	```
372	
373	### 4.1.7 Super-Saw / Unison Detuning
374	
375	The Roland JP-8000 "super saw" stacks multiple sawtooth oscillators with slightly detuned frequencies and random phase offsets. The characteristic wide sound comes from beating between the detuned partials.
376	
377	```typescript
378	interface UnisonConfig {
379	  voices: number;        // typically 3-9
380	  detune: number;        // cents, spread across voices (e.g., 30 cents)
381	  stereoSpread: number;  // 0-1, pan spread
382	}
383	
384	// Per voice: compute per-unison-oscillator frequency
385	function unisonFreqs(base: number, config: UnisonConfig): number[] {
386	  const { voices, detune } = config;
387	  return Array.from({ length: voices }, (_, i) => {
388	    const offset = voices > 1 ? (i / (voices - 1) - 0.5) * detune : 0;
389	    return base * Math.pow(2, offset / 1200); // cents to ratio
390	  });
391	}
392	```
393	
394	The summed signal is normalized by `1 / sqrt(voices)` to maintain consistent perceived loudness regardless of voice count.
395

397	
398	## 4.2 Filter Design
399	
400	### 4.2.1 Biquad Filter Coefficients
401	
402	The biquad filter transfer function (two poles, two zeros):
403	
404	```
405	H(z) = (b0 + b1*z^{-1} + b2*z^{-2}) / (1 + a1*z^{-1} + a2*z^{-2})
406	```
407	
408	RBJ Audio EQ Cookbook (Robert Bristow-Johnson) provides design equations for all standard filter types from three parameters: cutoff frequency fc (Hz), sample rate Fs, and quality factor Q.
409	
410	Intermediate variables:
411	```
412	w0 = 2*pi*fc/Fs
413	cos_w0 = cos(w0)
414	sin_w0 = sin(w0)
415	alpha = sin_w0 / (2*Q)
416	```
417	
418	**Lowpass:**
419	```
420	b0 = (1 - cos_w0) / 2
421	b1 = 1 - cos_w0
422	b2 = (1 - cos_w0) / 2
423	a0 = 1 + alpha
424	a1 = -2 * cos_w0
425	a2 = 1 - alpha
426	```
427	(All coefficients divided by a0 for normalized form)
428	
429	**Highpass:**
430	```
431	b0 = (1 + cos_w0) / 2
432	b1 = -(1 + cos_w0)
433	b2 = (1 + cos_w0) / 2
434	a0 = 1 + alpha
435	a1 = -2 * cos_w0
436	a2 = 1 - alpha
437	```
438	
439	**Bandpass (constant 0 dB peak):**
440	```
441	b0 = sin_w0 / 2
442	b1 = 0
443	b2 = -sin_w0 / 2
444	a0 = 1 + alpha
445	a1 = -2 * cos_w0
446	a2 = 1 - alpha
447	```
448	
449	**Notch (band-reject):**
450	```
451	b0 = 1
452	b1 = -2 * cos_w0
453	b2 = 1
454	a0 = 1 + alpha
455	a1 = -2 * cos_w0
456	a2 = 1 - alpha
457	```
458	
459	**Peak EQ:**
460	```
461	A = sqrt(10^(dBgain/20)) = 10^(dBgain/40)
462	b0 = 1 + alpha * A
463	b1 = -2 * cos_w0
464	b2 = 1 - alpha * A
465	a0 = 1 + alpha / A
466	a1 = -2 * cos_w0
467	a2 = 1 - alpha / A
468	```
469	
470	**Low shelf:**
471	```
472	b0 = A * [(A+1) - (A-1)*cos_w0 + 2*sqrt(A)*alpha]
473	b1 = 2*A * [(A-1) - (A+1)*cos_w0]
474	b2 = A * [(A+1) - (A-1)*cos_w0 - 2*sqrt(A)*alpha]
475	a0 = (A+1) + (A-1)*cos_w0 + 2*sqrt(A)*alpha
476	a1 = -2 * [(A-1) + (A+1)*cos_w0]
477	a2 = (A+1) + (A-1)*cos_w0 - 2*sqrt(A)*alpha
478	```
479	
480	TypeScript implementation with coefficient update:
481	
482	```typescript
483	interface BiquadCoeffs {
484	  b0: number; b1: number; b2: number;
485	  a1: number; a2: number;  // a0-normalized
486	}
487	
488	class BiquadFilter {
489	  private x1 = 0; private x2 = 0;
490	  private y1 = 0; private y2 = 0;
491	  private coeffs: BiquadCoeffs = { b0: 1, b1: 0, b2: 0, a1: 0, a2: 0 };
492	
493	  setLowpass(fc: number, Q: number, sampleRate: number): void {
494	    const w0 = (2 * Math.PI * fc) / sampleRate;
495	    const cosW0 = Math.cos(w0);
496	    const sinW0 = Math.sin(w0);
497	    const alpha = sinW0 / (2 * Q);
498	    const a0inv = 1 / (1 + alpha);
499	    this.coeffs = {
500	      b0: ((1 - cosW0) / 2) * a0inv,
501	      b1: (1 - cosW0) * a0inv,
502	      b2: ((1 - cosW0) / 2) * a0inv,
503	      a1: (-2 * cosW0) * a0inv,
504	      a2: (1 - alpha) * a0inv,
505	    };
506	  }
507	
508	  process(x: number): number {
509	    const { b0, b1, b2, a1, a2 } = this.coeffs;
510	    const y = b0 * x + b1 * this.x1 + b2 * this.x2
511	                     - a1 * this.y1 - a2 * this.y2;
512	    this.x2 = this.x1; this.x1 = x;
513	    this.y2 = this.y1; this.y1 = y;
514	    return y;
515	  }
516	}
517	```
518	
519	### 4.2.2 State-Variable Filter (SVF)
520	
521	The SVF topology (Chamberlin, 1979) simultaneously computes lowpass, highpass, and bandpass outputs from a single two-integrator loop. This makes it ideal for morphable filter types.
522	
523	Chamberlin SVF difference equations:
524	
525	```
526	f1 = 2 * sin(pi * fc / Fs)   // frequency coefficient (small fc approximation valid below Fs/6)
527	q1 = 1 / Q
528	
529	// Per sample:
530	LP = LP + f1 * BP
531	HP = input - LP - q1 * BP
532	BP = f1 * HP + BP
533	Notch = HP + LP
534	```
535	
536	The Chamberlin SVF has a stability limit: f1 must remain below approximately 0.5 (fc < Fs/6). The Hal Chamberlin (1985) and later the Zavalishin (2012) topology resolve this instability using the Zero Delay Feedback (ZDF) formulation.
537	
538	**ZDF State-Variable Filter (Zavalishin):**
539	
540	```
541	g = tan(pi * fc / Fs)    // prewarped frequency
542	k = 1 / Q
543	a1_coeff = 1 / (1 + g * (g + k))
544	a2_coeff = g * a1_coeff
545	a3_coeff = g * a2_coeff
546	
547	// Per sample (state variables ic1, ic2):
548	v3 = input - ic2
549	v1 = a1_coeff * ic1 + a2_coeff * v3
550	v2 = ic2 + a2_coeff * ic1 + a3_coeff * v3
551	
552	ic1 = 2 * v1 - ic1
553	ic2 = 2 * v2 - ic2
554	
555	HP = v3 - k * v1 - v2
556	BP = v1
557	LP = v2
558	Notch = HP + LP
559	AP = input - 2 * k * BP
560	```
561	
562	This ZDF formulation is stable at any cutoff up to Nyquist and is the preferred topology for professional synthesizer filters.
563	
564	### 4.2.3 Resonance and Self-Oscillation
565	
566	In a 2-pole SVF or ladder filter, resonance (Q > 1/sqrt(2) for a biquad, Q > 0.5 for SVF) produces a peak at the cutoff frequency. As Q increases, the peak height increases: the -3 dB point coincides with fc for Q = 0.707 (Butterworth), peaking higher for Q > 0.707.
567	
568	**Self-oscillation** occurs when the feedback gain equals unity, causing the filter to sustain a sinusoidal output at fc even with zero input. In a 4-pole Moog ladder, self-oscillation onset occurs at Q = 1/(4 * g * (1-g)^3) approximately. In practice, a dedicated "resonance" parameter is mapped nonlinearly so that maximum resonance approaches but does not exceed self-oscillation threshold, unless the designer explicitly wants self-oscillation as a feature.
569	
570	### 4.2.4 Moog Ladder Filter
571	
572	The Moog transistor ladder filter (Robert Moog, 1965) is a 4-pole (24 dB/octave) lowpass filter with feedback-controlled resonance. Its characteristic warm, nonlinear sound results from transistor saturation and feedback delay through the ladder stages.
573	
574	The Huovilainen (2004) model provides a computationally tractable digital approximation:
575	
576	```
577	// 4-stage ladder with nonlinear saturation (tanh per stage)
578	// State: y[0..3] (four stage outputs)
579	// thermal_v = 1/(2*VT), typically 1.22070313
580	
581	per sample:
582	  input_tanh = tanh(input - 4 * resonance * y[3])
583	  y0_new = y[0] + f * (tanh(input_tanh) - tanh(y[0]))
584	  y1_new = y[1] + f * (tanh(y0_new) - tanh(y[1]))
585	  y2_new = y[2] + f * (tanh(y1_new) - tanh(y[2]))
586	  y3_new = y[3] + f * (tanh(y2_new) - tanh(y[3]))
587	  // where f = 2 * sin(pi * fc / Fs)
588	```
589	
590	The Stilson & Smith (1996) model offers a linearized version that avoids tanh per sample (cheaper but less saturating character). The full nonlinear Huovilainen model is preferred for analog authenticity.
591	
592	### 4.2.5 Korg MS-20 Filter
593	
594	The MS-20 filter uses a Sallen-Key topology with diode-based nonlinear feedback. Its characteristic is a harsh, aggressive resonance that clips before self-oscillation. A simplified model:
595	
596	```
597	// Two-pole Sallen-Key with nonlinear feedback
598	per sample:
599	  feedback = clip(y1, -1, 1) * resonance * 4
600	  s1 = s1 + f * (tanh(input - feedback) - s1)
601	  s2 = s2 + f * (s1 - s2)
602	  y1 = s2
603	  // where clip limits the feedback path
604	```
605	
606	### 4.2.6 Web Audio API BiquadFilterNode vs Custom Filters
607	
608	The native `BiquadFilterNode` offers types: `lowpass`, `highpass`, `bandpass`, `lowshelf`, `highshelf`, `peaking`, `notch`, `allpass`. AudioParam scheduling gives sample-accurate frequency sweeps.
609	
610	**Limitations of BiquadFilterNode:**
611	- No self-oscillation
612	- No nonlinear saturation
613	- Fixed 12 dB/octave slope (2-pole only)
614	- No SVF simultaneous output access
615	- No per-sample coefficient modulation from JavaScript
616	
617	For production synthesizer filter modulation, a custom AudioWorklet filter is required. The Web Audio BiquadFilterNode is suitable only for static or slowly-varying EQ applications.
618

620	
621	## 4.3 Envelope Generators
622	
623	### 4.3.1 ADSR Architecture
624	
625	An ADSR envelope has four stages: Attack, Decay, Sustain, Release. State transitions:
626	
627	```
628	IDLE -> ATTACK (on note-on)
629	ATTACK -> DECAY (when level reaches 1.0)
630	DECAY -> SUSTAIN (when level reaches sustain level)
631	SUSTAIN -> RELEASE (on note-off)
632	RELEASE -> IDLE (when level reaches ~0)
633	```
634	
635	**Exponential segments** are perceptually superior to linear because the human ear perceives amplitude logarithmically. An exponential approach from current level to target:
636	
637	```
638	// Per sample, exponential approach:
639	level += (target - level) * coeff
640	// where coeff = 1 - exp(-1 / (timeInSamples * curveFactor))
641	```
642	
643	The `curveFactor` (typically 4-10) controls how "fast" the envelope approaches the target before slowing. A curveFactor of 0 gives linear; higher values give more exponential character.
644	
645	```typescript
646	class ADSREnvelope {
647	  private level = 0;
648	  private stage: 'idle' | 'attack' | 'decay' | 'sustain' | 'release' = 'idle';
649	
650	  // Time parameters in samples
651	  private attackCoeff = 0;
652	  private decayCoeff = 0;
653	  private releaseCoeff = 0;
654	  private sustainLevel = 0.7;
655	
656	  configure(attackMs: number, decayMs: number, sustainLevel: number, releaseMs: number, sampleRate: number): void {
657	    const curve = 0.001; // shape factor
658	    this.attackCoeff = this.timeToCoeff(attackMs * sampleRate / 1000, curve);
659	    this.decayCoeff = this.timeToCoeff(decayMs * sampleRate / 1000, curve);
660	    this.releaseCoeff = this.timeToCoeff(releaseMs * sampleRate / 1000, curve);
661	    this.sustainLevel = sustainLevel;
662	  }
663	
664	  private timeToCoeff(samples: number, curve: number): number {
665	    return 1 - Math.exp(-Math.log((1 + curve) / curve) / samples);
666	  }
667	
668	  noteOn(): void { this.stage = 'attack'; }
669	  noteOff(): void { if (this.stage !== 'idle') this.stage = 'release'; }
670	
671	  tick(): number {
672	    switch (this.stage) {
673	      case 'attack':
674	        this.level += this.attackCoeff * (1.0 + this.level * 0.3 - this.level);
675	        if (this.level >= 1.0) { this.level = 1.0; this.stage = 'decay'; }
676	        break;
677	      case 'decay':
678	        this.level += this.decayCoeff * (this.sustainLevel - this.level);
679	        break;
680	      case 'sustain':
681	        break;
682	      case 'release':
683	        this.level += this.releaseCoeff * (0.00001 - this.level);
684	        if (this.level <= 0.0001) { this.level = 0; this.stage = 'idle'; }
685	        break;
686	    }
687	    return this.level;
688	  }
689	}

4:summary: "Survey of synthesizer architecture, DSP algorithms, and sound design patterns for implementing professional-grade software synthesizers using the Web Audio API and AudioWorklet, including the iconic TR-808 drum machine."
16:The paper addresses ten interconnected domains: oscillator design including band-limited algorithms and wavetable synthesis; filter design spanning biquad, state-variable, and analog-modeled topologies; envelope generators with exponential and multi-stage curves; low-frequency oscillators with sync and tempo modes; modulation matrix architecture; the Roland TR-808 drum machine as a worked case study in analog synthesis emulation; arpeggiator patterns; polyphony management and voice allocation; preset serialization and morphing; and effects processing from convolution reverb through parametric EQ. Each domain receives mathematical foundations, algorithmic analysis, trade-off discussion, and concrete TypeScript/Web Audio API code patterns suitable for production implementation.

863:## 4.6 The TR-808 Drum Machine
867:The Roland TR-808 (1980) generates all drum sounds through analog synthesis -- no samples. Each instrument channel is a distinct analog circuit with fixed structure but voltage-controlled parameters (pitch, decay, accent). The step sequencer triggers these circuits.
1024:The TR-808 step sequencer runs 16 steps per pattern at a fixed tempo, with each step either active or inactive per voice. Two patterns (A and B) can be stored and chained.
1063:For the TR-808 emulation in a browser:
1608:The TR-808 case study demonstrates that analog drum synthesis emulation is achievable with a small number of oscillators, noise generators, and filters per voice -- the complexity lies in the accurate modeling of circuit-specific behaviors (exponential pitch envelopes, specific inharmonic oscillator ratios, mutual-exclusivity of hi-hat circuits) rather than in computational power.

Perfect! I now have comprehensive knowledge of the codebase. Let me compile the detailed report.

## DAW Codebase Exploration Report

I've thoroughly explored the long-running-harness DAW codebase. Here's a comprehensive analysis of its architecture and key components:

---

### 1. **Overall Project Structure**

The codebase follows a layered, modular architecture with clear separation of concerns:

```
src/
├── audio/              # Web Audio API engine (E3, E7, E9, E4, E2)
│   ├── synth/          # Synthesizer (E9)
│   ├── mixer/          # Mixer core (E7)
│   ├── media-pool/     # Audio sample management (E4)
│   ├── effects/        # Effects rack system
│   ├── transport-clock.ts      # Main transport clock (E3)
│   ├── look-ahead-scheduler.ts # Real-time scheduler
│   └── [other audio primitives]
├── ui/                 # React components (E2)
│   ├── controls/       # Knobs, faders, buttons
│   ├── primitives/     # Base UI components
│   ├── tokens/         # Design tokens (colors, spacing, fonts)
│   ├── synth/          # Synth UI editor
│   ├── mixer/          # Mixer panel
│   ├── media-pool/     # Media browser UI
│   └── [other panels]
├── state/              # Zustand store + session management
│   ├── store.ts        # Main DAW store
│   ├── track/          # Track/clip state
│   ├── synth/          # Synth preset state
│   └── session/        # Session persistence
└── test/               # Test utilities
```

**Key Path Aliases:** `@/`, `@audio/`, `@ui/`, `@state/` (enforced by ESLint)

---

### 2. **Synthesizer Implementation (E9)**

**File:** `/src/audio/synth/`

The synthesizer is a **16-voice polyphonic engine** with:

#### Architecture:
- **Voice allocation:** Oldest-steal with 5ms crossfade, 2ms minimum release (MIT-H4-5, MIT-H4-6)
- **Per-voice signal chain:**
  - 2x PolyBLEP oscillators (sine, saw, square, triangle)
  - Biquad filter (lowpass, highpass, bandpass)
  - ADSR amplitude envelope
  - ADSR filter envelope
  - Glide (pitch portamento)
- **Shared:** 2x LFOs (hardwired modulation matrix)

#### Key Files:
- **`synth-types.ts`**: Parameter map (oscillators, filter, envelopes, LFOs), voice commands, MIDI-to-frequency conversion
- **`synth-processor.ts`** (11KB): AudioWorklet processor — runs on audio thread, zero-allocation `process()`
- **`synth-instrument.ts`**: Main-thread controller, creates AudioWorkletNode, sends commands via MessagePort

#### DSP Components (in `dsp/`):
- **`polyblep.ts`** (105 lines): PolyBLEP oscillator with O(1) antialiasing
  - Corrects discontinuities in sawtooth/square waves
  - Achieves quality up to ~8kHz fundamental at 48kHz
  - Used for all non-sine waveforms

- **`adsr.ts`** (95 lines): ADSR envelope with exponential curves
  - Attack, decay, sustain, release stages
  - Exponential approach for perceptually smooth transitions

- **`biquad-coeffs.ts`** (115 lines): RBJ Audio EQ Cookbook filter design
  - Lowpass, highpass, bandpass coefficient computation
  - Direct Form I implementation

- **`lfo.ts`** (80 lines): Low-frequency oscillator
  - Sine, square, triangle, sample-and-hold shapes
  - Tempo-sync capable

- **`voice-allocator.ts`** (250 lines): Polyphony manager
  - Tracks 16 voices with states (idle, allocated, stealing)
  - Handles note-on/off, legato mode, crossfade overlap

**Default Parameters:** 
- OSC1: Saw, 0.8 level
- OSC2: Saw, 7 cents detune, 0.5 level
- Filter: LP, 5kHz cutoff, Q=1
- Amp envelope: 10ms attack, 200ms decay, 70% sustain, 300ms release
- Master gain: 0.7

---

### 3. **Transport & Scheduling Engine (E3)**

**Files:** `/src/audio/transport-clock.ts`, `/src/audio/look-ahead-scheduler.ts`

#### TransportClock:
A state machine that manages playback position with **lock-free SharedArrayBuffer communication**:

```typescript
type TransportClock = {
  state: "stopped" | "playing" | "paused"
  play() / pause() / stop() / seek()
  setBpm() / setLoop()
  getCursorSeconds()      // Pure read
  updateCursor()          // Advances state + writes SAB
  didLoopWrap(): boolean  // Detects loop wraps
}
```

**Key Features:**
- **Lock-free SAB reads:** UI thread reads cursor position, BPM, state without locks
- **Precision playback:** Uses `AudioContext.currentTime` for sample-accurate scheduling
- **Loop support:** `LoopRegion` with wrap detection and re-sync
- **Tempo map:** Supports bar/beat/tick (BBT) position queries

**Implementation Details:**
- SAB layout: cursor (Float64), state (Uint8), BPM (Float32)
- Play position = `playStartCursorSeconds + (ctx.currentTime - playStartContextTime)`
- Loop wrap triggers `loopWrapped = true`, re-syncs playback point

#### LookAheadScheduler:
A **real-time scheduler** that queues events ahead of playback:

```typescript
type LookAheadScheduler = {
  scheduleEvent(atSeconds: number, msg: EngineCommand): void
  tick(): void  // Fires events within lookahead window
}
```

**Lookahead mechanism:**
- Default 100ms ahead of playback cursor
- Prevents early-quantum triggering artifacts
- Asynchronous message posting to avoid jitter

#### Subscription API:
Both components expose reactive updates via:
- **MessagePort** for asynchronous main-thread ← → audio-thread communication
- **SharedArrayBuffer** for lock-free state reads
- **Zustand store** (UI layer) subscribes to cursor updates

---

### 4. **Mixer Core (E7)**

**Files:** `/src/audio/mixer/mixer-engine.ts`, `/src/audio/mixer/types.ts`

#### Audio Graph Architecture:
```
[Track Sources] → [ChannelStrip (per-track)]
  ├─ inputGain
  ├─ insertChain (dry → effects → wet)
  ├─ faderGain (logarithmic taper)
  ├─ panner (stereo)
  ├─ muteGain (solo logic)
  └─ analyser (metering)
                      ↓
                 [Master Bus]
                  ├─ inputGain
                  ├─ faderGain
                  ├─ limiter (DynamicsCompressor, INV-2)
                  ├─ analyser
                  └─ ctx.destination
```

#### Channel Strip (`ChannelStrip` type):
- **trackId**: Unique identifier
- **inputGain**: Pre-insert level control
- **inserts**: Ordered array of effect slots (can be bypassed)
- **faderGain**: Post-insert level with quadratic taper for natural feel
- **panner**: StereoPannerNode (-1 to 1)
- **muteGain**: Implements solo/mute logic atomically
- **analyser**: FFT for metering UI
- **Mute/Solo state:** `muted`, `solo`, `soloIsolate` booleans

#### Master Bus:
- **INV-2 Invariant:** Non-bypassable DynamicsCompressorNode as brickwall limiter

   - Amplitude envelope (user-controlled decay 200–900ms)
   - Click transient at onset

2. **Snare Drum (SD):**
   - Tuned sine (~180 Hz) for "crack"
   - White noise through bandpass filter (~1 kHz, Q=1.5)
   - Separate fast/slow envelopes for tone/noise mix

3. **Hi-Hats (Open/Closed):**
   - Six square oscillators at inharmonic ratios: [1, 1.342, 1.563, 1.842, 2.127, 2.537]
   - Base frequency: 400 Hz
   - Bandpass/highpass filter (~8–10 kHz)
   - Closed hat: 6–50ms decay; Open hat: 200ms–1s decay
   - **Mutual exclusivity:** Triggering CH cuts OH immediately (shared envelope)

4. **Clap (CP):**
   - White noise, bandpass filtered (~1 kHz)
   - Four bursts at 0, 8, 16, 24ms offsets
   - Each burst has separate envelope (last longest)

5. **Additional Voices:**
   - **Cowbell (CB):** Two square oscillators (540 Hz, 800 Hz), short decay
   - **Rimshot (RS):** Brief noise + sine tone (~100 Hz), high-freq click
   - **Toms (LT, MT, HT):** Pitch-enveloped sine oscillators, higher frequencies

```typescript
interface TR808Pattern {
  steps: boolean[][]        // [voice][step], 16 steps per voice
  accent: boolean[]         // per-step accent flag
  tempo: number             // BPM
  swing: number             // 0–1 amount of swing
}

- Swing applies time offset to even steps
- Accent boosts amplitude by ~6 dB + slight decay extension

### 10. **Key Architectural Patterns**

#### A. **Zero-Allocation Audio Processing** (INV-3)
- All DSP classes pre-allocate state in constructor
- `process()` functions never call `new` or allocate memory
- Critical for AudioWorklet real-time stability

#### B. **Lock-Free Synchronization**
- SharedArrayBuffer for cursor position, BPM, state
- Main thread reads without waiting; audio thread writes atomically
- Avoids audio thread blocking

#### C. **AudioWorklet Message Protocol**
- Main thread → WorkletNode via `port.postMessage(command)`
- Worklet → Main thread via `port.postMessage(response)`
- Latency ~100ms typical (asynchronous, decoupled from audio)
- Sample-accurate scheduling via `audioCtx.currentTime + lookahead`

#### D. **Modular Effect System** (`/src/audio/effects/`)
- Factory pattern: `EffectFactory` creates `EffectInstance`
- Registry: lookup by `typeId` (e.g., "reverb", "delay", "distortion")
- Preset schema: Zod validation for parameter serialization
- Dry/wet mixing: `dryGain` and `wetGain` nodes per effect

#### E. **State Management** (Zustand)
- Single `useDawStore` hook manages:
  - Transport (play/pause/stop, BPM, cursor, loop)
  - Tracks & clips (add, remove, move, split, duplicate)
  - Selection (selected track IDs, clip IDs)
  - Master volume
- Persist via `session/use-session-persistence.ts` → IndexedDB
- Undo/redo via `state/undo/shared-undo-manager.ts`

- Auto-save every 5 seconds (configurable)
- Recovery dialog on app startup if crash detected
- In-memory storage option for testing

- `AudioEngineProvider`: Makes engine available to UI
- `MediaPoolProvider`: Makes media pool available to UI
- `TransportProvider`: Provides transport clock to schedulers
- `EffectsBridgeProvider`: Connects UI to effects state

**File:** `/src/audio/synth/`

- **Voice allocation:** Oldest-steal with 5ms crossfade, 2ms minimum release (MIT-H4-5, MIT-H4-6)
- **Per-voice signal chain:**
  - 2x PolyBLEP oscillators (sine, saw, square, triangle)
  - Biquad filter (lowpass, highpass, bandpass)

  - Corrects discontinuities in sawtooth/square waves
  - Achieves quality up to ~8kHz fundamental at 48kHz
  - Used for all non-sine waveforms

- **`adsr.ts`** (95 lines): ADSR envelope with exponential curves
  - Attack, decay, sustain, release stages
  - Exponential approach for perceptually smooth transitions

- **`biquad-coeffs.ts`** (115 lines): RBJ Audio EQ Cookbook filter design
  - Lowpass, highpass, bandpass coefficient computation
  - Direct Form I implementation

- **`lfo.ts`** (80 lines): Low-frequency oscillator
  - Sine, square, triangle, sample-and-hold shapes
  - Tempo-sync capable

- **`voice-allocator.ts`** (250 lines): Polyphony manager
  - Tracks 16 voices with states (idle, allocated, stealing)
  - Handles note-on/off, legato mode, crossfade overlap

**Default Parameters:** 
- OSC1: Saw, 0.8 level
- OSC2: Saw, 7 cents detune, 0.5 level
- Filter: LP, 5kHz cutoff, Q=1
- Amp envelope: 10ms attack, 200ms decay, 70% sustain, 300ms release

### 3. **Transport & Scheduling Engine (E3)**

**Files:** `/src/audio/transport-clock.ts`, `/src/audio/look-ahead-scheduler.ts`

#### TransportClock:
A state machine that manages playback position with **lock-free SharedArrayBuffer communication**:

```typescript
type TransportClock = {
  state: "stopped" | "playing" | "paused"
  play() / pause() / stop() / seek()
  setBpm() / setLoop()
  getCursorSeconds()      // Pure read
  updateCursor()          // Advances state + writes SAB
  didLoopWrap(): boolean  // Detects loop wraps
}
```

**Key Features:**
- **Lock-free SAB reads:** UI thread reads cursor position, BPM, state without locks
- **Precision playback:** Uses `AudioContext.currentTime` for sample-accurate scheduling
- **Loop support:** `LoopRegion` with wrap detection and re-sync
- **Tempo map:** Supports bar/beat/tick (BBT) position queries

**Implementation Details:**
- SAB layout: cursor (Float64), state (Uint8), BPM (Float32)
- Play position = `playStartCursorSeconds + (ctx.currentTime - playStartContextTime)`
- Loop wrap triggers `loopWrapped = true`, re-syncs playback point

#### LookAheadScheduler:
A **real-time scheduler** that queues events ahead of playback:

```typescript
type LookAheadScheduler = {
  scheduleEvent(atSeconds: number, msg: EngineCommand): void
  tick(): void  // Fires events within lookahead window
}
```

**Lookahead mechanism:**
- Default 100ms ahead of playback cursor
- Prevents early-quantum triggering artifacts
- Asynchronous message posting to avoid jitter

#### Subscription API:
Both components expose reactive updates via:
- **MessagePort** for asynchronous main-thread ← → audio-thread communication
- **SharedArrayBuffer** for lock-free state reads
- **Zustand store** (UI layer) subscribes to cursor updates

   - Closed hat: 6–50ms decay; Open hat: 200ms–1s decay
   - **Mutual exclusivity:** Triggering CH cuts OH immediately (shared envelope)

4. **Clap (CP):**
   - White noise, bandpass filtered (~1 kHz)
   - Four bursts at 0, 8, 16, 24ms offsets
   - Each burst has separate envelope (last longest)

5. **Additional Voices:**
   - **Cowbell (CB):** Two square oscillators (540 Hz, 800 Hz), short decay
   - **Rimshot (RS):** Brief noise + sine tone (~100 Hz), high-freq click
   - **Toms (LT, MT, HT):** Pitch-enveloped sine oscillators, higher frequencies

#### Step Sequencer (Research 4.6.7):
```typescript
interface TR808Pattern {
  steps: boolean[][]        // [voice][step], 16 steps per voice
  accent: boolean[]         // per-step accent flag
  tempo: number             // BPM
  swing: number             // 0–1 amount of swing
}
```
- Swing applies time offset to even steps
- Accent boosts amplitude by ~6 dB + slight decay extension
- Fixed 16-step-per-pattern structure

---

### 9. **Test Structure & Patterns**

**Vitest Configuration:** `/vitest.config.ts`
- Environment: `jsdom` (DOM simulation)
- Setup: `./src/test/setup.ts` (imports `@testing-library/jest-dom/vitest`)
- Globals: `true` (describe, it, expect available without imports)
- Coverage: v8, 80+ test files across the codebase

**Test Patterns:**

1. **Unit Tests for DSP:**
   - `/src/audio/synth/dsp/polyblep.test.ts` — oscillator output validation
   - `/src/audio/synth/dsp/adsr.test.ts` — envelope stage transitions
   - `/src/audio/synth/dsp/voice-allocator.test.ts` — allocation/stealing logic
   - Input ranges, boundary conditions, state transitions

2. **Integration Tests:**
   - `/src/audio/transport-integration.test.ts` — playback loop/seek interactions
   - `/src/audio/mixer/mixer-engine.test.ts` — strip creation, mute/solo logic

3. **React Component Tests:**
   - `/src/ui/controls/RotaryKnob.test.tsx` — mouse drag, keyboard, rendering
   - `/src/ui/primitives/Button.test.tsx` — click callbacks, disabled states
   - `/src/ui/transport/TransportBar.test.tsx` — playback controls

4. **Test Conventions:**
   - Colocated with source (`*.test.ts` next to `*.ts`)
   - TDD-friendly (tests first, implementation follows)
   - No `any` types; strict TypeScript in tests
   - Use `@testing-library/react` for component testing

**Test Command:**
```bash
pnpm test              # Run once
pnpm test:watch       # Watch mode
pnpm test:coverage    # Coverage report (v8 provider)
```

---

### 10. **Key Architectural Patterns**

#### A. **Zero-Allocation Audio Processing** (INV-3)
- All DSP classes pre-allocate state in constructor
- `process()` functions never call `new` or allocate memory
- Critical for AudioWorklet real-time stability

#### B. **Lock-Free Synchronization**
- SharedArrayBuffer for cursor position, BPM, state
- Main thread reads without waiting; audio thread writes atomically
- Avoids audio thread blocking

#### C. **AudioWorklet Message Protocol**
- Main thread → WorkletNode via `port.postMessage(command)`
- Worklet → Main thread via `port.postMessage(response)`
- Latency ~100ms typical (asynchronous, decoupled from audio)
- Sample-accurate scheduling via `audioCtx.currentTime + lookahead`

#### D. **Modular Effect System** (`/src/audio/effects/`)
- Factory pattern: `EffectFactory` creates `EffectInstance`
- Registry: lookup by `typeId` (e.g., "reverb", "delay", "distortion")
- Preset schema: Zod validation for parameter serialization
- Dry/wet mixing: `dryGain` and `wetGain` nodes per effect

#### E. **State Management** (Zustand)
- Single `useDawStore` hook manages:
  - Transport (play/pause/stop, BPM, cursor, loop)
  - Tracks & clips (add, remove, move, split, duplicate)
  - Selection (selected track IDs, clip IDs)
  - Master volume
- Persist via `session/use-session-persistence.ts` → IndexedDB
- Undo/redo via `state/undo/shared-undo-manager.ts`

#### F. **Session Persistence**
- Auto-save every 5 seconds (configurable)
- Recovery dialog on app startup if crash detected
- In-memory storage option for testing

#### G. **React Context Providers**
- `AudioEngineProvider`: Makes engine available to UI
- `MediaPoolProvider`: Makes media pool available to UI
- `TransportProvider`: Provides transport clock to schedulers
- `EffectsBridgeProvider`: Connects UI to effects state

### 11. **Code Quality & Standards**

**Tooling:**
- **TypeScript 5.8:** Strict mode, `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`, `exactOptionalPropertyTypes`
- **ESLint 10 + typescript-eslint:** No `any`, enforce path aliases
- **Prettier:** Automated formatting
- **Husky:** Pre-commit linting + format checks
- **GitHub Actions CI:** Lint, format, test, build on PR

**Conventions:**
- **Naming:** camelCase for functions/variables, PascalCase for types/components
- **Exports:** Use `export` not `export default` (except components)
- **Comments:** Multi-line JSDoc for public APIs
- **Error Handling:** Discriminated unions (e.g., `{ ok: true; handle: ... } | { ok: false; error: ... }`)

# TR-808 Drum Machine (E10) -- Code Review

## Summary

The implementation delivers a sample-based 808 drum kit engine, a 16-step pattern sequencer with A/B storage, and a panel UI with per-instrument step rows, pads, and parameter knobs. The architecture follows established codebase patterns (factory functions, `connectToMixer` convention, separation of audio engine from UI). Total test count is 42 across 4 test files.

The code is generally clean and well-structured. The findings below are organized by severity.

---

## Must Fix

### 1. `onSetAccent` and `onSetFlam` props are accepted but never wired to UI controls

**File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/src/ui/drum-machine/DrumMachinePanel.tsx`

The `DrumMachinePanelProps` type declares `onSetAccent` and `onSetFlam` callbacks (lines 22-23), but the component destructuring at line 137 omits them entirely -- they are never passed through to any child element. There is no UI affordance (button, checkbox, right-click menu, etc.) for a user to set accent or flam on any step.

This means the epic requirements for "per-step accent toggle" and "per-step flam timing" are incomplete at the UI layer. The audio engine and sequencer correctly support these features, but the user has no way to interact with them.

**Recommended fix**: Add minimal UI for accent/flam. Possibilities ranked by simplicity:
- (a) Right-click (context menu) on a StepButton to toggle accent; shift+right-click to set flam
- (b) A small accent indicator row below each instrument row
- (c) An accent toggle button per step, only visible for the "currently selected" instrument

### 2. `setParam` has no input validation/clamping

**File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/src/audio/drum-machine/drum-kit.ts`, line 110-117

The `setParam` method writes values directly without any range validation:

```typescript
setParam(instrumentId, key, value): void {
  const p = params.get(instrumentId);
  if (!p) return;
  (p as Record<string, number>)[key] = value;
}
```

The types document valid ranges (tone: 200-20000, decay: 0.01-2, tune: 0.5-2, volume: 0-1), and the UI sliders enforce these ranges, but the audio engine itself does not. If `setParam` is called programmatically (e.g., from a future preset loader or MIDI controller mapping) with out-of-range values:
- `decay` at 0 or negative would cause `exponentialRampToValueAtTime` to target a value at `time + 0`, producing audible glitches
- `volume` above 1.0 combined with `velocity` of 1.0 would produce clipping
- `tone` at 0 Hz would make the BiquadFilter unstable

**Recommended fix**: Add a 3-line clamping helper or clamp inline in `setParam`. The ranges are already defined implicitly in the UI `ParamKnob` min/max values; they should be codified as constants in `drum-types.ts` and enforced in the engine.

---

## Should Improve

### 3. `disconnectFromMixer` disconnects the entire output node, not just the mixer target

**File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/src/audio/drum-machine/drum-kit.ts`, lines 125-127

```typescript
disconnectFromMixer(_mixer: MixerEngine, _trackId: string): void {
  output.disconnect();
}
```

`output.disconnect()` with no arguments disconnects from ALL destinations. If the drum kit output were ever connected to multiple destinations (e.g., a send bus or analyzer), this would sever all connections. The synth-instrument.ts has the exact same pattern (`node.disconnect()`), so this is a pre-existing codebase convention -- but it is worth noting as tech debt.

**Recommended fix**: For architectural consistency with future send/bus routing, consider `output.disconnect(strip.inputGain)` using `mixer.getStrip(trackId)` to get the specific destination. However, if the current convention is intentionally simple (one output destination per instrument), this can remain as-is with a comment explaining the assumption.

### 4. Missing barrel exports from parent modules

**File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/src/audio/index.ts` -- no drum-machine exports
**File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/src/ui/index.ts` -- no DrumMachinePanel export

The audio barrel (`src/audio/index.ts`) does not re-export anything from `@audio/drum-machine`. The UI barrel (`src/ui/index.ts`) does not re-export `DrumMachinePanel`. Every other module in the codebase (synth, mixer, transport, media-pool, controls) is surfaced through these barrels. This means consumers must use deep imports (`@audio/drum-machine`) instead of the canonical barrel path (`@audio`).

**Recommended fix**: Add export lines to both barrel files, following the established patterns already present for mixer, synth, and controls.

### 5. Hi-hat mutual exclusivity test is weak

**File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/src/audio/drum-machine/drum-kit.test.ts`, lines 91-96

```typescript
it("hi-hat mutual exclusivity: CH cuts OH", () => {
  kit.trigger("oh", 0.1, 0.8);
  kit.trigger("ch", 0.15, 0.8);
  expect(ctx.createBufferSource).toHaveBeenCalled();
});
```

This test only asserts that `createBufferSource` was called, which is trivially true for any trigger call. It does not verify that the OH voice was actually stopped/cut. A stronger test would assert that the OH gain node's `cancelScheduledValues` and `setValueAtTime(0)` were called, or that the OH source's `stop()` was invoked. Without this, the mutual exclusivity feature could regress undetected.

**Recommended fix**: Capture the gain node mock returned from the first `createGain()` call (the OH voice's gain) and assert that `gain.cancelScheduledValues` and `source.stop` were called after the CH trigger.

### 6. `accent boosts velocity` test does not verify the velocity value

**File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/src/audio/drum-machine/drum-kit.test.ts`, lines 129-133

```typescript
it("accent boosts velocity", () => {
  kit.trigger("sd", 0.1, 1.0);
  expect(ctx.createBufferSource).toHaveBeenCalled();
});
```

This test triggers with velocity 1.0 directly, which is the accent velocity. But it does not verify that the gain envelope peak was set to `volume * 1.0` vs. the normal `volume * 0.8`. It is effectively testing that `trigger` does not crash, not that accent produces louder output. The real accent logic lives in the step sequencer (which is tested), but the kit-level test name is misleading.

**Recommended fix**: Either rename to "trigger with high velocity creates source" (accurate description) or assert on the gain value passed to `setValueAtTime`.

---

## Could Enhance

### 7. No sample loading mechanism

The `createDrumKit` function takes a pre-populated `Map<DrumInstrumentId, AudioBuffer>` of samples, but there is no utility function for loading samples from URLs or files. The epic specifies "sample-based 808 with one-shot samples" but the implementation defers sample loading to the consumer.

This is architecturally fine (separation of concerns), but a `loadDrumSamples(ctx, urls: Record<DrumInstrumentId, string>): Promise<Map<...>>` helper would be a natural companion export.

### 8. Pattern switching has no cross-fade or timing guard

**File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/src/audio/drum-machine/step-sequencer.ts`, line 110-114

`switchPattern` immediately changes the active pattern. If called mid-step (between `scheduleStep` calls from the look-ahead scheduler), there is no mechanism to defer the switch to the next bar boundary or even the next step. This could cause mid-beat pattern jumps. The epic does not specify boundary-aligned switching, so this is enhancement-level only.

### 9. `DrumStep.triggers` type uses `Record<DrumInstrumentId, boolean>` but step is `readonly`

**File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/src/audio/drum-machine/drum-types.ts`, lines 75-80

The `DrumStep` type marks `triggers` as `readonly`, but in `step-sequencer.ts` the mutable working copy uses `{ ...step.triggers }` spread to create new objects for mutation. This is correct in practice, but the sequencer internally stores `DrumStep[]` (mutable array of readonly objects), which requires the spread-copy dance on every toggle. A small type refinement (e.g., a `MutableDrumStep` internal type) would make the intent clearer.

### 10. Inline styles in DrumMachinePanel

The panel component uses extensive inline `style` objects rather than CSS modules, which is inconsistent with the existing UI controls (`StepButton` and `DrumPad` both use `.module.css` files). This makes the component harder to theme and increases bundle size due to repeated style object allocations.

### 11. ParamKnob uses `<input type="range">` rather than the existing `RotaryKnob` component

**File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/src/ui/drum-machine/DrumMachinePanel.tsx`, lines 44-78

The codebase already exports a `RotaryKnob` component from `@ui/controls/RotaryKnob`. Using native range inputs instead is functional but visually inconsistent with the rest of the DAW UI.

---

## Epic Requirements Checklist

| Requirement | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Sample-based 808 (NOT analog synthesis) | PASS | AudioBufferSourceNode playback |
| 11 drum instruments (BD, SD, LT, MT, HT, RS, CP, CB, OH, CH, CY) | PASS | All present in `DRUM_INSTRUMENTS` |
| Per-instrument Tone (BiquadFilter cutoff) | PASS | lowpass filter, cutoff set at trigger time |
| Per-instrument Decay (amplitude envelope release) | PASS | exponentialRampToValueAtTime |
| Per-instrument Tune (playbackRate) | PASS | setValueAtTime on playbackRate |
| Per-instrument Volume (GainNode gain) | PASS | Peak = volume * velocity |
| Sample playback via AudioBufferSourceNode + BiquadFilter + GainNode | PASS | Chain: source -> filter -> gain -> output |
| 16-step sequencer grid UI with per-step on/off toggle | PASS | StepButton per instrument per step |
| Per-step accent toggle | PARTIAL | Engine supports it; no UI control to set it |
| Per-step flam timing | PARTIAL | Engine supports it; no UI control to set it |
| Pattern A/B storage and switching | PASS | Two patterns, A/B buttons in header |
| Step sequencer synced to transport clock | PASS | `scheduleStep(stepIndex, time)` callback model |
| Audio output routed to mixer drum track | PASS | `connectToMixer` matches synth convention |
| 808 panel UI: pad grid (trigger on click) | PASS | DrumPad per instrument |
| Step sequencer row per instrument | PASS | InstrumentRow component |
| Per-instrument knobs | PASS | ParamKnob (Tone, Decay, Tune, Vol) |
| Interface exports: DrumKit, DrumPattern, DrumTrigger | PASS | All in index.ts |

---

## Prioritized Action Plan

1. **[Must Fix]** Wire `onSetAccent` and `onSetFlam` to actual UI affordances in `DrumMachinePanel.tsx`, or the accent/flam features are unreachable by users.

2. **[Must Fix]** Add parameter clamping in `drum-kit.ts` `setParam()` to prevent out-of-range values from producing audio glitches. Extract range constants to `drum-types.ts`.

3. **[Should Improve]** Strengthen the hi-hat mutual exclusivity test and the accent velocity test in `drum-kit.test.ts` to actually verify the behaviors they claim to test.

4. **[Should Improve]** Add drum-machine exports to `src/audio/index.ts` and `DrumMachinePanel` to `src/ui/index.ts` to match barrel export conventions.

5. **[Could Enhance]** Consider switching inline styles to CSS modules and native range inputs to `RotaryKnob` for visual consistency with the rest of the DAW UI.

---
